text.title: Agent Dev Tuning: Session Patterns and Workflows
text.summary: tmux session setup with linger, git worktree workflow for parallel agents, resource-limited agent launcher, Claude Code settings.json, and pre-flight check script.

rule.statement: Enable `loginctl enable-linger` for the agent user so that tmux and systemd user services start at boot and survive SSH disconnects. Without linger, the user session ends on logout and all agent processes die.
rule.rationale: When SSH disconnects, the login session ends and SIGHUP is sent to all processes in that session. Linger keeps a persistent user session alive at all times, so tmux survives reboots and disconnects without a screen/nohup workaround.

example.code:
# Enable linger (run once, as root or the user) loginctl enable-linger nero
# Verify loginctl show-user nero | grep Linger # Linger=yes
# Create a named tmux session for agent work tmux new-session -d -s work -n "claude" tmux new-window -t work -n "logs" tmux new-window -t work -n "monitor"
# Attach tmux attach -t work

section.title: Git Worktree Workflow for Parallel Agents

rule.statement: Use `git worktree add` to give each parallel agent its own directory on a separate branch. Never have two agents on the same branch simultaneously — they will overwrite each other's uncommitted changes.
rule.rationale: Git worktrees share the object store but each has its own index and HEAD. This means two agents can stage and commit changes independently without any locking or conflict, while sharing the full git history.

example.code:
# Create worktree for a task git worktree add ../nero-core-task-123 -b agent/task-123
# Agent works in the worktree cd ../nero-core-task-123 # ... agent makes changes, commits ...
# List active worktrees git worktree list
# Remove after merging git worktree remove ../nero-core-task-123 git branch -d agent/task-123
# Prune stale worktrees (after the directory was deleted externally) git worktree prune

rule.statement: Use `systemd-run --user --scope` to apply `MemoryMax` and `CPUQuota` per agent session. This prevents one runaway agent from starving other services.
rule.rationale: Claude Code's LLM API response parsing can spike to 2–4 GB RAM temporarily. Without a cgroup scope, that spike competes with PostgreSQL and the main API service. A per-agent scope caps the spike and preserves system stability.

example.code: # Launch Claude Code with resource limits in a named cgroup scope systemd-run --user --scope \ --unit="claude-agent-1" \ --property=MemoryMax=4G \ --property=CPUQuota=400% \ -- tmux new-session -d -s agent-1 claude

section.title: Claude Code settings.json Allow List
section.p: A reasonable allow list for an agent doing infrastructure and Python development work. Place in `~/.claude/settings.json` or the project `.claude/settings.json`.

see-also.link: Claude Code settings.json with allow list
tmux session setup: work + logs + monitor
Verify all tuning is applied, print OK/LOW per item
